coredata.dc:title: Determination of dopamine in human serum based on green-emitting fluorescence carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Meng-Xia
Wei, Na
Pang, Lan-Fang
Guo, Xiao-Feng
Wang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Through facile and straightforward hydrothermal treatment of quercetin, green-emitting carbon dots (g-CDs) were synthesized. The as-prepared g-CDs exhibited good water solubility and stability. Moreover, the strong fluorescence of obtained g-CDs at 535 nm could be quenched by dopamine (DA), which was proven to be effective in detecting DA ranging from 2.0 to 40.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.52 μM. Additionally, the g-CDs further showed its feasibility for DA determination in human serum with satisfactory recoveries (95.58%–106.87%), indicating that the g-CDs can be used as a promising tool for the detection of DA in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Green-emitting
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design thoughts and choice of materials
Selectivity of the proposed method for DA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21777126 and 32070398 , Beijing, China).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the g-CDs; (b) Particle size distribution histogram of g-CDs; (c) XRD pattern of g-CDs; (d) FTIR spectrum of g-CDs.
(a–b) XPS spectra of the g-CDs; (c) The absorption spectrum of g-CDs; (d) Fluorescent emission spectra of g-CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 355 nm to 445 nm.

(a) Fluorescence decay trace of g-CDs; (b–c) The fluorescence intensity of g-CDs versus from pH and temperature; (d) The fluorescence intensity of g-CDs after continuous irradiation of 100 W incandescent lamp for 12 h.
(a) The effect of pH on fluorescence quenching efficiency; (b) The effect of reaction time and reaction temperature on the fluorescence response of the g-CDs and DA.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the g-CDs upon addition of various concentrations of DA. (from top to bottom, 0 and 100 μM DA, inset shows the fluorescence quenching efficiency as a function of DA concentration); (b) The fluorescence quenching efficiency as a function of DA concentration of 2–40 μM. (c) Fluorescence response of g-CDs in the presence of various analytes. 1 to 28 represent Blank, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Pro, Gly, His, Trp, Tyr, Ser, Arg, Glu, Thr, Lys, Cys, Phe, GSH, Glc, Lac, Man, Fru, l-dopa, AA, UA, EP, and DA, respectively.
(a–b) FTIR spectra and fluorescence lifetime curves of before (black line) and after (red line) reacting with DA; (c) Fluorescence response of g-CDs in the presence of DA and OX-DA respectively.
Comparison of different indicator for DA detection.
The analysis of DA in actual samples.
Comparison of different method for the analysis of DA in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Determination of dopamine in human serum based on green-emitting fluorescence carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan, 430072, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Through facile and straightforward hydrothermal treatment of quercetin, green-emitting carbon dots (g-CDs) were synthesized. The as-prepared g-CDs exhibited good water solubility and stability. Moreover, the strong fluorescence of obtained g-CDs at 535 nm could be quenched by dopamine (DA), which was proven to be effective in detecting DA ranging from 2.0 to 40.0 μM with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.52 μM. Additionally, the g-CDs further showed its feasibility for DA determination in human serum with satisfactory recoveries (95.58%–106.87%), indicating that the g-CDs can be used as a promising tool for the detection of DA in human serum.

Fabrication of green-emitting carbon dots (g-CDs) using quercetin as precursor, the fluorescence of the g-CDs can be effectively quenched by dopamine (DA).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), a neurotransmitter naturally secreted by the brain, has great influence on the operation of the central nervous, hormonal, and cardiovascular systems [1]. Fluctuations of DA level in the blood can be used as a signal for the occurrence of diseases like Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease and Schizophrenia [2]. Considering the serious influence on body health, developing methods for simple and effective detection of DA in the blood is very necessary.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a fluorescent nanomaterial emerging in 2004, have received growing research attention in environmental detection, gene transmission, drug carriers, electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, biosensing and bioimaging for its excellent properties like good solubility in water, outstanding optical properties, free heavy metal in composition, and low cost [3–9]. At present, many researchers have used CDs to construct sensors. Possessing excellent electrochemical performance enables CDs to be used in the field of electrochemical sensing [10]. Modifying the electrode with CDs can make the electrode surface have catalytic performance to improve the selectivity and sensitivity of the electrode [11]. The currently reported electrochemical sensors can selectively respond to DA without interference from ascorbic acid and uric acid through the modification of CDs [12–16]. Meanwhile, their optical properties are also commonly applied for sensing [17]. Since changes in the physical and chemical environment may affect the optical properties (absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum) of CDs, CDs can be used as colorimetric and fluorescent sensors [18–20]. The colorimetric sensors can do fast, simple and even naked eye detection, but are relatively less sensitive [20–22]. For the sensing of DA, comparing the currently reported colorimetric and fluorescent strategies based on CDs, the sensitivity of the latter is usually higher [23–27]. Currently, some CDs-based fluorescent probes have been exploited to detect DA. For instance, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots [28,29] and Fe-doped carbon quantum dots [30] were capable of acting as probes for DA determination owing to the electron transfer between the CDs and DA oxidation product dopamine-quinone (DA-quinone). The CDs obtained via pyrolyzing lactose could respond to DA through the polymerization of DA on the CDs and thus be used for DA analysis [31]. Nitrogen and boron co-doped carbon quantum dots [32] and boronic acid-functionalized CDs [2,33,34] have been adopted as effective probes to determine DA due to forming the complex of DA and CDs. Using tyrosinase as an oxidant, the CDs conjugated with tyrosinase showed good performance in DA detection [35]. Moreover, the CDs fabricated from H3PO4 and ethylenediamine have been prepared to detect Cr(VI) and DA via an on-off-on strategy [36]. Besides, some hybrid materials probes including CDs-gold nanoclusters [37], CDs/copper nanoclusters [38], CDs and Cu–Mn–O microcrystals [24] have also been applied to the analysis of DA. However, the fluorescence emission of the above-mentioned CDs is mostly around 460 nm [2,28–31,33–36], and the highest is 490 nm [32], which can be easily interfered by background absorption and autofluorescence in complex samples. In addition, a portion of probes require complicated experimental set-ups and operations [24,33–38]. Thus, further effort is necessary to develop easy-to-implement CDs with green or even longer emission for DA detection.

Herein, taking both sensitivity and emission wavelength into account, green-emitting CDs (g-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using quercetin as precursor. The fluorescence of g-CDs at 535 nm could be efficiently quenched by DA under alkaline conditions, attributing to electron transfer between g-CDs and DA oxidation product DA-quinone. The g-CDs can generate linear response to DA ranging from 2.0 to 40.0 μM with a low LOD of 0.52 μM. The proposed g-CDs further showed its feasibility for determining DA in human serum samples with satisfactory results. So, a feasible strategy to use the g-CDs as the easy-to-implement probe for DA determination was built in this work.

Quercetin and levodopa (l-dopa) were purchased from Energy Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). DA, glutathione (GSH), and twelve kinds of amino acids including Proline (Pro), Glycine (Gly), histidine (His), Tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr), Serine (Ser), Arginine (Arg), glutamic acid (Glu), Threonine (Thr), Lysine (Lys), Cysteine (Cys), Phenylalanine (Phe) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Lactose (Lac), glucose (Glc), mannose (Man), fructose (Fru), Uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), quinine sulfate, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) were obtained from Sinopharm Reagent (Shanghai, China). Epinephrine (EP) was purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Human serums were obtained from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water (18.25 M Ω cm) was used throughout the entire experimental process.

The observation of g-CDs morphology was done using JEM2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL Ltd., Japan). The crystal structure of g-CDs was known by Miniflex 600 X-ray power diffraction (PXRD) (Rigaku, Japan). IS10 fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) was carried out to identify the functional groups on the surface of g-CDs. The elemental composition of g-CDs was obtained by Escalab250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) (Thermo, America). The fluorescence lifetime decay curves and fluorescence spectra (FL) of the g-CDs were analyzed using FLS1000 (Edinburgh, Britain) and RF-6000 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The absorption spectra were tested using UV-4802 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Unico, China).

We successfully synthesized green-emitting CDs via the hydrothermal carbonization of quercetin under alkaline conditions. Briefly, 100 mg quercetin was dissolved in 10 mL NaOH (0.1 M) in a beaker under stirring, then the uniform solution was heated in a Teflon-lined stainless steel hydrothermal autoclave at 180 °C for 12 h. Brown solution was obtained after cooling down to the room temperature. Finally, the resultant solution was freeze-dried to obtain powdery CDs and stored at 4 °C for subsequent applications.

The quantum yield (QY) of g-CDs was measured using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 58%) as a reference standard and determined through following the equation [39]:
(1)
φ =
φ S
(
(
I
A s
η 2
)
/
(
I A
η s 2
)
)
Where, “∅” refers to the QY, “I” is the integrated fluorescence intensity, “A” represents the absorbance which is guaranteed to be not higher than 0.05, “η” is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” stands for the standard reference of known QY.

The entire experimental process for DA detection was repeated at least three times to acquire trusted results. Solutions with different concentrations of DA (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 80, 100 μM) were added to 0.1 mg/mL standard solution of g-CDs prepared with phosphate buffer (PB, 100 mM, pH = 9), and the mixture was incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. After the reaction was completed, fluorescence intensity was recorded at 375/535 nm.

Sample preparation was performed according to the literature [40], but changed a little. Acetonitrile was added to the serum samples (the volume of acetonitrile and serum was 2:1), so that large molecules and proteins could be precipitated. Then the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min at room temperature. The supernatant was collected and stored at −20 °C for subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design thoughts and choice of materials
ce:section.ce:para: Donor-acceptor systems have been widely used for the construction of novel probes in detecting target [41]. Under alkaline conditions, DA can be oxidized into an excellent electron acceptor, DA-quinone [42]. Meanwhile, CDs serve as either electron donors or electron acceptors depending on the conditions [43]. Therefore, in order to obtain CDs with considerable electron-donating ability to establish donor-acceptor system for DA analysis, we chose quercetin possessing a majority of electron-donating phenolic hydroxyl groups as the precursor, and conducted the following experiments. Acetic acid, ethanol, and NaOH solution (0.1 M) were used respectively as the solvent of quercetin to synthesize CDs. And, it was found that the water-solubility of the CDs prepared in NaOH solution was the best. The preliminary results showed the green fluorescence of g-CDs can be effectively quenched by DA. This is in line with what we anticipated.

So as to explore the structural and morphological composition of the g-CDs, various techniques were employed to characterize the g-CDs. The microstructure information of the g-CDs was investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 a displays clearly the TEM image of g-CDs, from which we can figure out g-CDs are monodispersed. The size distribution (Fig. 1b) indicates that the diameters of g-CDs are in the range of 1.2–2.9 nm with an average size about 2.01 nm.

The broad peak around 26° corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials points out the crystallization degree and graphene-like structures of g-CDs in Fig. 1c attained by XRD, which is in accordance with TEM results [44].

Employing FTIR spectrophotometer, the surface functional groups of the g-CDs were further explored (Fig. 1d). The typical absorption band located at 3447 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of O–H. The characteristic peak around 2921 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of C–H. The absorption peaks at 1653 cm−1 and 1362 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations CO and C–O–C [45,46]. Two peaks appearing at 1157 cm−1 and 1074 cm−1 reveal the existence of C–O [45].

The XPS clearly verifies the elemental composition of g-CDs. Two peaks appear at 284.8 eV and 531.5 eV in the full-scan XPS survey (Fig. 2 a), which are respectively corresponded to C 1s, O 1s [47]. Fig. 2b explains C 1s spectra, which shows four peaks related to CC (284.45 eV), C–C (285.10 eV), C–O (286.20 eV), CO (288.75 eV) [3].

The optical properties of g-CDs were investigated through absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. In general, the optical absorption of CDs is located at UV region with a tail extending to visible region [48]. The g-CDs have three characteristic absorption peaks (Fig. 2c). The first absorption peak at around 253 nm is due to π-π* transitions of conjugated CC system, while the second and third absorption peak at around 350 nm and 430 nm are involved with n-π* transitions of CO surface groups [49,50]. The fluorescence spectrum indicates that the maximum emission peak at 535 nm appears with the 375 nm excitation (Fig. 2d). Moreover, the emission peak of g-CDs is still at 535 nm when the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 340 nm to 480 nm (Fig. 2d). The QY of g-CDs was determined to be 2.4% with quinine sulfate as the reference. Through fitting the time resolve fluorescence decay plot of the g-CDs, the average fluorescence lifetime of g-CDs was calculated to be 5.79 ns (Fig. 3 a).

The stability of the g-CDs was assessed using the change of fluorescence intensity as an indicator at different pH, temperature, and long-term photo irradiation. The fluorescence intensity of g-CDs is relatively weak in an acidic environment, but it remains strong and relatively constant in pH the range of 7–11 (Fig. 3b). What caused the fluorescence intensity of g-CDs to be significantly different in acidic and alkaline environments is the change in the surface charge of g-CDs caused by protonation-deprotonation [51]. The fluorescence intensity of g-CDs remains constant when the temperature changes ranging from 4 °C to 55 °C, indicating the satisfactory temperature-stability of the g-CDs (Fig. 3c). For the photo-stability, we continuously irradiated the g-CDs for 12 h with a 100 W bulb in 30 cm distance. The results in Fig. 3d show that the fluorescence of the g-CDs remains almost unchanged after long-term photo irradiation, which reveals the considerable photo-stability of g-CDs.

Experimental parameters, including incubation time, pH of the reaction system, and reaction temperature in the detection system, were optimized. First, we investigated the most suitable pH for the DA quenching system (Fig. 4 a). With the increasing of pH from 8 to 11, the fluorescence intensity of the g-CDs decreases at first and then increases. This shows suitable pH is instrumental in attaining good analytical performance, and pH 9 is the best. What explains the result is too low or too high proton concentration is not conducive to the oxidation of DA to DA-quinone.

We further optimized reaction temperature and incubation time. As shown in Fig. 4b, 37 °C and 20 min are appropriate to achieve the highest fluorescence quenching efficiency for the detection system. The result may due to the effect of temperature on the oxidation of DA to DA-quinone.

In the detection system, the fluorescence intensity of the g-CDs shows gradual quenching toward the increase in DA content (Fig. 5 a). The fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DA present a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9932) in the range of 2–40 μM with 0.52 μM as the LOD (signal to noise ratio of 3). Compared to other fluorescent probes in Table 1 , the analysis performance of g-CDs on sensitivity and wavelength is considerable. The linear regression equation is (F0–F)/F0 = 0.01031 + 0.01607 [DA], where F0 and F respectively correspond to the fluorescence intensity of the g-CDs in the absence and presence of DA (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed method for DA
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the g-CDs to DA was investigated by the selective experiment. The fluorescence intensity of g-CDs incubated with normal ions, common substances, and DA similar substances (Ca2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Pro, Gly, His, Trp, Tyr, Ser, Arg, Glu, Thr, Lys, Cys, Phe, GSH, UA, Glc, Lac, Man, Fru, AA, EP, l-dopa, DA, and the concentration of Mg2+, Fe3+, Cys, GSH, AA, EP, l-dopa, DA was 40 μM while others were 200 μM) was measured. The results in Fig. 5c show that other substances cannot cause significant fluctuations in the fluorescence intensity of g-CDs except for DA and EP. However, in practical, EP is synthesized from DA, and it is found in serum at low concentrations [52]. So, EP cannot disturb the DA detection in serum. Therefore, above information reveals that the g-CDs can specifically respond to DA in complex real samples.

At present, electron transfer has been considered as one of the important causes of fluorescence quenching of fluorophores. The phenomenon that the fluorescence of the CDs are quenched by DA through the electron transport between the DA and CDs in alkaline environment has been reported [28,53,54]. The fluorescence quenching of CDs caused by electron transfer belongs to dynamic quenching. However, when dynamic quenching occurs, the quencher does not react with the CDs, and the FTIR spectra of CDs will not change [55]. So, to confirm the quenching mechanism between DA and g-CDs, FTIR spectra of g-CDs in the absence and presence of DA were investigated first. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 6 a show that none new groups appear on the surface of the g-CDs in the presence of DA, which indicates that the fluorescence quenching of g-CDs is not caused by the formation of new groups. In the dynamic quenching process, the collision between the CDs and the quencher molecules makes the excited state of CDs lose their excitation energy and return to the ground state through the energy transfer mechanism or the charge transfer mechanism. Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs will change [56]. Then, the mechanism was further investigated with the fluorescence lifetime decays of g-CDs in the absence and presence of DA. The obtained results (Fig. 6b) show, the time resolved fluorescence decay curves of g-CDs in the absence and presence of DA have a significant change. And the average fluorescence lifetime of g-CDs in the absence and presence of DA respectively are 5.79 ns and 4.95 ns. The significant change (0.84 ns) in the fluorescence lifetime of g-CDs is a strong evidence for dynamic quenching and the occurrence of electron transfer between g-CDs and DA [57] (Fig. 6b).

An interference test of oxidized dopamine was executed to clarify whether DA-quinone quenched the fluorescence of g-CDs. The result in Fig. 6c illustrates that oxidized DA is well consistent with the quenching performance of DA. Therefore, it is well verified that electron transfer occurring in g-CDs and DA quenches the fluorescence of g-CDs.

The standard addition method used in DA detection in real samples demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed method. Known concentrations of DA (20, 30, and 40 μM) were added to 100-fold diluted human serum samples. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of DA were in the range of 95.58%–106.87%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) of DA were lower than 1.4%. The above results illuminate that the serum samples have no significant effect on the detection of DA in the detection system, and the detection system is promising for the detection of DA in real samples. Compared with other methods for the analysis of DA in actual samples in Table 3 , the proposed method on analysis time, recovery and RSD are considerable.

In summary, green-emission CDs were prepared through a facile hydrothermal procedure. The obtained g-CDs are well soluble and dispersed in water, as well as stable. The g-CDs were in response to DA via a fluorescent quenching approach and the quenching mechanism was clearly revealed. The g-CDs in DA determination have both considerable sensitivity and emission wavelength. Moreover, the g-CDs have been successfully used for the assay of DA in serum samples with recoveries of 95.58%–106.87%, and the construction of assay system and the sample pre-treatment are simple, which indicates the g-CDs have a promising prospect in DA detection in real applications in future.

Hong Wang: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing-review & editing. Meng-Xia Wei: Investigation, Methodology, Writing-original draft preparation, Writing-review & editing. Xiao-Feng Guo: Conceptualization, Resources, Methodology, Writing-review & editing, Funding acquisition. Na Wei: X-ray power diffraction characterization. Lan-Fang Pang: Infrared spectroscopy characterization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21777126 and 32070398, Beijing, China).
